title: Cracking the Code of Shakespeare's Language
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Thou art confused. The words look like English but don't sound like it. The sentences are backward. Everyone else seems to understand, and you feel like you're reading a foreign language. Here's the secret: Shakespeare isn't Old English (that's Beowulf). It's Early Modern English — just English with different word order and a few strange words. You already know how to understand it. You just need a few tricks.

The Biggest Difference: Word Order

Shakespeare often puts verbs before subjects and objects in unusual places. He does this for rhythm and rhyme. Your job is to unscramble the sentence into normal English order: subject → verb → object.

Example: 'To be or not to be, that is the question.' Normal order would be: 'That is the question: to be or not to be.' Same words. Different order. Now it makes sense.

When you hit a confusing line, ask: who is doing what to whom? Rearrange the words until you find the answer.

Thee, Thou, Thy, Thine (Not as Hard as They Seem)

These are just old-fashioned versions of 'you' and 'your.' There's a pattern:
• Thou = you (subject) — 'Thou art' = 'you are'
• Thee = you (object) — 'I give thee' = 'I give you'
• Thy = your (before a consonant sound) — 'thy hand' = 'your hand'
• Thine = your (before a vowel sound) — 'thine eyes' = 'your eyes'

Shakespeare also uses these to show relationships. 'Thou' is informal or intimate. 'You' is formal or respectful. When characters switch between them, it tells you something about their relationship.

Verbs Ending in -est and -eth

• -est is for 'thou' (you): 'thou speakest' = 'you speak'
• -eth or -th is for he/she/it: 'he speaketh' = 'he speaks'

That's it. Just old-fashioned verb endings. Ignore them or translate them mentally. 'Doth' = 'does.' 'Hath' = 'has.' 'Dost' = 'do.' You'll see these constantly.

Contractions You've Never Seen

Shakespeare invented words and squished others together. Common ones:
• 'tis = it is
• o'er = over
• e'er = ever
• ne'er = never
• a' = he (short for 'he' or 'it')
• i' = in

Once you know these, the text becomes much more readable.

Read It Out Loud (Seriously)

Shakespeare wrote plays to be performed, not read silently in a classroom. The lines are meant to be spoken. Read them out loud. You'll hear the rhythm, the emotion, and the meaning in ways your eyes miss.

Overact. Be dramatic. Put emotion into the words. You'll be surprised how much clearer the meaning becomes.

Use the Footnotes (They're There to Help You)

Your textbook has footnotes for a reason. They explain the archaic words, the puns, and the cultural references. Use them. There's no prize for avoiding the footnotes.

If you're reading online, No Fear Shakespeare has the original text on one side and a modern translation on the other. Use it to check your understanding, not as a replacement for reading the original.

Watch the Play First

Before you read a scene, watch a performance (movie or stage recording). Seeing the actors' expressions, tone, and physicality will help you understand what's happening. Then read the text. You'll spend less time figuring out the plot and more time appreciating the language.

Kenneth Branagh's adaptations are excellent. The BBC also has filmed stage productions. Even a student production on YouTube is better than nothing.

Remember: It's Supposed to Be Funny (or Sad, or Angry)

Shakespeare isn't a museum piece. It's entertainment. The jokes are funny (even if they're 400 years old). The insults are creative. The tragedies are genuinely moving. If you're bored, you're missing the performance. Imagine the scene. Hear the characters. Feel the emotion.

You don't have to understand every word. Get the gist. Get the emotion. Get the story. The rest is details — important details, but details.

Shakespeare is learnable. Millions of students have survived him. You will too. One scene at a time.
